content.content:
1 import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; 2 3 export const hvmPhases: Phase[] = [ 4 { 5 id: "what", 6 heading: "What Hivemind is", 7 body: ( 8 <> 9

10 Hivemind is strategic analytical software for operators reasoning under 11 uncertainty — founders, analysts, and decision-makers who have to commit 12 to a course of action when the evidence is incomplete and the stakes are 13 real. 14

15

16 The one-line pitch: instead of a memo full of confident-sounding prose, 17 you get a structured argument you can defend, attack, and update — with 18 the assumptions named and the evidence attached. 19

20 21 ), 22 }, 23 { 24 id: "problem", 25 heading: "What problem it solves", 26 body: ( 27 <> 28

29 Most strategic deliberation fails the same way. A team writes a memo, 30 fluent prose hides the load-bearing assumptions, and nobody can argue 31 with it because there is nothing concrete to argue against. The team 32 commits anyway, and when reality disagrees, no one can point at the 33 step where the reasoning broke. 34

35

36 Hivemind targets that failure. The working artifact is not a paragraph 37 — it is a tree of claims with sources attached and a confidence number 38 on each. When the answer changes, you can point at the claim that 39 moved, and so can the next person who picks up the file. 40

41 42 ), 43 }, 44 { 45 id: "how", 46 heading: "How it works, briefly", 47 body: ( 48 <> 49

50 You drop a strategy question in as plain prose. Hivemind reads it for 51 the entities and decisions involved and breaks it into smaller 52 sub-claims you can actually evaluate one at a time. 53

54

55 You attach evidence to each branch — citations weighted by how 56 trustworthy and how recent the source is. Underneath, the software 57 keeps a running confidence number for every claim and rolls those up 58 to the question at the top. Above, you get a working surface: a tree 59 you can walk, edit, and challenge. 60

61

62 The output is a structured brief with citations and confidence 63 intervals — not a chat transcript. The reasoning travels with the 64 conclusion. 65

66 67 ), 68 }, 69 { 70 id: "status", 71 heading: "Where it sits", 72 body: ( 73 <> 74

75 Hivemind is in active development at seed stage. Question 76 decomposition, the evidence ledger, and the end-to-end workflow are 77 usable today. Scenario branching and counterfactual simulation are in 78 alpha against a small set of operator partners. Adversarial review and 79 the final briefing render are scoped but not yet shipping. 80

81

82 For deeper questions, ask the assistant on /chat, 83 or keep reading the project page below for the full breakdown. 84

85 86 ), 87 }, 88 ]; 89 90 // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P02 §B1). 91 if (hvmPhases.length !== 4) { 92 throw new Error( 93 `hvm.tsx: hvmPhases.length must be 4 (got ${hvmPhases.length})`, 94 ); 95 } 96

file.content:
import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; export const hvmPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "what", heading: "What Hivemind is", body: ( <>

Hivemind is strategic analytical software for operators reasoning under uncertainty — founders, analysts, and decision-makers who have to commit to a course of action when the evidence is incomplete and the stakes are real.

The one-line pitch: instead of a memo full of confident-sounding prose, you get a structured argument you can defend, attack, and update — with the assumptions named and the evidence attached.

), }, { id: "problem", heading: "What problem it solves", body: ( <>

Most strategic deliberation fails the same way. A team writes a memo, fluent prose hides the load-bearing assumptions, and nobody can argue with it because there is nothing concrete to argue against. The team commits anyway, and when reality disagrees, no one can point at the step where the reasoning broke.

Hivemind targets that failure. The working artifact is not a paragraph — it is a tree of claims with sources attached and a confidence number on each. When the answer changes, you can point at the claim that moved, and so can the next person who picks up the file.

), }, { id: "how", heading: "How it works, briefly", body: ( <>

You drop a strategy question in as plain prose. Hivemind reads it for the entities and decisions involved and breaks it into smaller sub-claims you can actually evaluate one at a time.

You attach evidence to each branch — citations weighted by how trustworthy and how recent the source is. Underneath, the software keeps a running confidence number for every claim and rolls those up to the question at the top. Above, you get a working surface: a tree you can walk, edit, and challenge.

The output is a structured brief with citations and confidence intervals — not a chat transcript. The reasoning travels with the conclusion.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Where it sits", body: ( <>

Hivemind is in active development at seed stage. Question decomposition, the evidence ledger, and the end-to-end workflow are usable today. Scenario branching and counterfactual simulation are in alpha against a small set of operator partners. Adversarial review and the final briefing render are scoped but not yet shipping.

For deeper questions, ask the assistant on /chat, or keep reading the project page below for the full breakdown.

), }, ]; // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P02 §B1). if (hvmPhases.length !== 4) { throw new Error( `hvm.tsx: hvmPhases.length must be 4 (got ${hvmPhases.length})`, ); }

content.content:
1	// TODO(michael): replace with the real preface
2	const preface = [
3	  "This book began as a refusal. The refusal was simple: to keep producing inside systems whose only consistent output was the perpetuation of themselves. The refusal was also, more uncomfortably, a confession. I was good at producing inside those systems. The confession is what made the book necessary.",
4	  "Purposeless Efficiency is the first of five volumes. It examines what efficiency means once it has been severed from any external purpose — once the metric is the meaning. The remaining volumes will treat what comes after the severance: the institutions that survive it, the ones that do not, and what an honest economic order might look like once we admit the mistake.",
5	  "Reader, I assume you have already noticed the symptoms. The book is for the reader who has stopped pretending the symptoms are accidental.",
6	];
7	export default preface;
8

1	// TODO(michael): rewrite each pillar body in your voice
2	export type Pillar = {
3	  num: "I" | "II" | "III" | "IV" | "V";
4	  title: string;
5	  seed: string;       // single-line collapsed quote
6	  body: string;       // expanded paragraph(s)
7	};
8	
9	const pillars: Pillar[] = [
10	  { num: "I",  title: "Corporatism",
11	    seed: "What it has done to work, and what it calls 'ambition.'",
12	    body: "Corporatism is the management of human attention by institutions whose primary product is no longer goods or services but the legitimation of their own continued existence. Ambition, under corporatism, is the willingness to mistake the institution's survival for one's own." },
13	  { num: "II", title: "Gamification",
14	    seed: "The industry of scoring, ranking, grinding; and its psychological residue.",
15	    body: "When every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the workflow. The optimization target eats the optimized object. Gamification is not a UX choice; it is a worldview." },
16	  { num: "III", title: "Incumbency",
17	    seed: "Why the machine does not die when it should.",
18	    body: "Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they are wired into the substrate of decision-making. Their replacement requires not better arguments but a different substrate." },
19	  { num: "IV", title: "Complacency",
20	    seed: "The citizen's withdrawal, and the elite's.",
21	    body: "Complacency is the rational response to a system whose levers have been disconnected from outcomes. The cost of paying attention exceeds the expected return on attention. The citizen withdraws; so, eventually, does the elite." },
22	  { num: "V",  title: "Economic Revolution",
23	    seed: "What comes next, and how we avoid the failure modes of the last one.",
24	    body: "Revolution requires a coherent alternative, not merely a critique. The alternative must be operable by humans of ordinary virtue, or it will be captured by humans of extraordinary ambition." },
25	];
26	export default pillars;
27

1	// Prose narrative for the PRP project page.
2	// Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content):
3	//   - preface.ts / preface.mdx → the refusal, the confession, "metric is the
4	//                                meaning", "the symptoms are not accidental"
5	//   - pillars.ts (I–V)         → corporatism, gamification, incumbency,
6	//                                 complacency, economic revolution
7	//   - arc.ts                   → five-volume arc; Book I status: writing
8	//   - progress.ts              → 92,143 / 100,000 words, manuscript v4
9	//   - quotes.ts                → severance, leaderboard-as-workflow,
10	//                                 incumbency wired into the substrate
11	// Per v2 principle 2 (content first), each section opens with its point.
12	
13	export type BodySection = {
14	  slug: string;
15	  heading: string;
16	  paragraphs: string[];
17	};
18	
19	const sections: BodySection[] = [
20	  {
21	    slug: "the-distinction",
22	    heading: "The distinction",
23	    paragraphs: [
24	      "Purposeless Efficiency is the name for a specific severance: efficiency cut loose from any external purpose, until the metric is the meaning. The book’s opening line is the cleanest statement of the argument — efficiency, severed from purpose, becomes the most efficient way of arriving nowhere. The distinction is not between efficient and inefficient systems. It is between systems that still remember what they are for and systems that have replaced their purpose with the measurement of themselves.",
25	      "The book’s claim is that this severance is not a local pathology of one industry or one institution. It is the dominant production logic of the late industrial era, and the symptoms — the sense that the levers no longer connect to outcomes, the sense that the leaderboard has eaten the work — are not accidental.",
26	    ],
27	  },
28	  {
29	    slug: "why-it-matters",
30	    heading: "Why it matters",
31	    paragraphs: [
32	      "The reader, the preface assumes, has already noticed the symptoms. The book is written for the reader who has stopped pretending they are accidental. The wager is that naming the severance precisely — distinguishing optimization from purpose, metric from meaning — is a precondition for any honest response to it. Critique that misdescribes the disease prescribes the wrong medicine.",
33	      "The stakes are not aesthetic. When every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the workflow, and the optimization target eats the optimized object. The institution survives; the thing it was supposed to do does not. Ambition, under these conditions, becomes the willingness to mistake the institution’s survival for one’s own. That substitution is what the book is trying to make visible enough to refuse.",
34	    ],
35	  },
36	  {
37	    slug: "where-it-shows-up",
38	    heading: "Where it shows up",
39	    paragraphs: [
40	      "The manuscript organizes the symptoms into five pillars. The first is corporatism: the management of human attention by institutions whose primary product is no longer goods or services but the legitimation of their own continued existence. The second is gamification: when every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the workflow; the optimization target eats the optimized object, and the worldview follows.",
41	      "The third pillar is incumbency. Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they are wired into the substrate of decision-making; their replacement requires not better arguments but a different substrate. The fourth is complacency: the rational response to a system whose levers have been disconnected from outcomes. The cost of paying attention exceeds the expected return on attention; the citizen withdraws, and so, eventually, does the elite.",
42	      "The fifth pillar turns from diagnosis to prescription: the shape of an economic revolution that does not repeat the failure modes of the last one. Revolution, the book argues, requires a coherent alternative rather than a critique. The alternative must be operable by humans of ordinary virtue, or it will be captured by humans of extraordinary ambition.",
43	    ],
44	  },
45	  {
46	    slug: "what-the-manuscript-covers",
47	    heading: "What the manuscript covers",
48	    paragraphs: [
49	      "Book I is the diagnostic volume. It opens with a preface — the book began as a refusal, and as a confession, that I was good at producing inside systems whose only consistent output was the perpetuation of themselves — and walks the five pillars in turn, building the argument from corporatism through to the conditions under which an economic revolution would be honest rather than performative.",
50	      "Purposeless Efficiency is Book I of a five-volume series. The remaining volumes treat what comes after the severance: the institutions that survive it, the ones that do not, and what an honest economic order might look like once we admit the mistake. Working titles for Books II through V are provisional; the volumes are in planning, not drafting.",
51	    ],
52	  },
53	  {
54	    slug: "status",
55	    heading: "Status",
56	    paragraphs: [
57	      "Book I is in active writing on manuscript version four, at roughly 92,000 of a target 100,000 words. Books II through V are scoped at the volume level and remain in planning with provisional titles. There is no public release date and no publisher announcement; the page exists to make the argument legible while the manuscript continues. Readers who want to be notified when the book is ready can use the notify list on the project record.",
58	    ],
59	  },
60	];
61	
62	export default sections;
63

1	// Used by the landing teaser and (optionally) the book page.
2	const quotes = [
3	  "Efficiency, severed from purpose, becomes the most efficient way of arriving nowhere.",
4	  "The leaderboard becomes the workflow; the workflow becomes the worldview.",
5	  "Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they are wired into the substrate of decision-making.",
6	];
7	export default quotes;
8

content.text: I have all the source content. Now I'll create the two files.

input.content:
import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/preface.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/pillars.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/arc.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/progress.ts // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/quotes.ts export const prpPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "distinction", heading: "The distinction", body: ( <>

Purposeless efficiency is efficiency severed from any external purpose, until the metric is the meaning. One concrete example: a workflow that has been turned into a leaderboard, where the leaderboard becomes the workflow and the worldview follows.

Purposeful efficiency still remembers what it is for; it optimises in service of something outside itself. One concrete example: an institution whose primary product remains the goods or services it was built to deliver, rather than the legitimation of its own continued existence.

), }, { id: "why-it-matters", heading: "Why it matters", body: ( <>

When purposeless efficiency goes unrecognised, the optimization target eats the optimized object. The first case is gamification: every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the workflow, and the institution survives while the thing it was supposed to do does not.

The second case is incumbency and the complacency that follows from it. Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they are wired into the substrate of decision-making. Given levers disconnected from outcomes, the rational response is to stop pulling — the cost of paying attention exceeds the expected return, the citizen withdraws, and so, eventually, does the elite.

), }, { id: "manuscript", heading: "What I'm doing about it", body: ( <>

I'm writing a manuscript called Purposeless Efficiency — Book I of a planned five-volume series. Book I is the diagnostic volume. It opens with a refusal and a confession — that I was good at producing inside systems whose only consistent output was their own perpetuation — and then walks five pillars in turn: corporatism, gamification, incumbency, complacency, and economic revolution.

The remaining four volumes will treat what comes after the severance: the institutions that survive it, the ones that do not, and what an honest economic order might look like once we admit the mistake. Working titles for Books II through V are provisional; the volumes are scoped at the volume level and remain in planning, not drafting.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Status & where to go", body: ( <>

Book I is in active writing on manuscript version four, at roughly 92,000 of a target 100,000 words. Books II through V remain in planning. There is no public release date and no publisher announcement; this page exists to make the argument legible while the manuscript continues.

For the longer breakdown, keep reading the project page below. For questions, ask the assistant on /chat.

), }, ]; // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P03 §B1). if (prpPhases.length !== 4) { throw new Error( `prp.tsx: prpPhases.length must be 4 (got ${prpPhases.length})`, ); }
